questions.question: The primary structure of a protein is:

options.B: Sequence of amino acids linked by peptide bonds

questions.question: Vitamin C deficiency causes:

options.B: Scurvy (defective collagen synthesis)

questions.question: Which mineral is a component of haemoglobin?

options.B: Iron (Fe2+ in haem group)

questions.question: Fat-soluble vitamins include:

options.B: Vitamins A, D, E, K

questions.question: The quaternary structure of protein refers to:

options.B: Assembly of two or more polypeptide subunits (e.g., haemoglobin with 4 subunits)
options.C: Alpha helix and beta sheet formation

questions.question: Vitamin D deficiency in children causes:

options.B: Rickets (softening and deformation of bones)

questions.question: The diagram shows the four levels of protein structure. What type of bonds maintain secondary structure?
questions.diagram_description: Diagram showing four levels: (1) Primary – linear amino acid chain connected by peptide bonds; (2) Secondary – alpha helix (spiral, maintained by H-bonds between backbone NH and CO groups 4 residues apart) and beta sheet (parallel/antiparallel chains, H-bonds between strands); (3) Tertiary – 3D folding of entire polypeptide (H-bonds, ionic bonds, disulfide bridges, hydrophobic interactions); (4) Quaternary – multiple polypeptide subunits (haemoglobin – 2 alpha, 2 beta chains)

options.B: Hydrogen bonds between backbone groups

questions.question: Iodine is essential for synthesis of:

options.B: Thyroid hormones (T3 and T4)

questions.question: Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) deficiency causes:

options.B: Pernicious anaemia and neurological damage

questions.question: Disulfide bonds in protein structure are formed between:

options.B: Cysteine residues (-SH groups oxidized to -S-S-)

questions.question: The diagram shows denaturation of a protein. What happens to primary structure during denaturation?
questions.diagram_description: Diagram showing native protein (properly folded, active conformation) and denatured protein (unfolded, inactive). Denaturation causes: unfolding of tertiary and secondary structure (H-bonds, hydrophobic interactions broken), loss of biological activity. Primary structure (peptide bonds) INTACT. Caused by: heat, pH extremes, detergents, urea. Renaturation possible in some proteins (e.g., ribonuclease A). Denatured protein shown as random coil

options.A: Primary structure is destroyed (peptide bonds broken)
options.B: Primary structure (amino acid sequence) remains intact; only 3D folding is disrupted
options.C: All bonds including peptide bonds are broken

questions.question: Pellagra is caused by deficiency of:

options.B: Vitamin B3 (Niacin)

questions.question: Calcium in the body is regulated mainly by:

options.B: PTH, Vitamin D (calcitriol), and calcitonin

questions.question: Zinc deficiency leads to:

options.B: Growth retardation, immune dysfunction, impaired wound healing

questions.question: Tertiary structure of proteins is stabilized by:

options.B: Hydrogen bonds, ionic bonds, disulfide bridges, and hydrophobic interactions

questions.question: The diagram shows the structure of an alpha helix. How many amino acids are there per turn?
questions.diagram_description: Diagram of alpha helix showing: right-handed helix, amino acid residues shown as spheres, backbone spiral, hydrogen bonds shown as dotted lines between N-H of one residue and C=O of residue 4 positions ahead (i to i+4 H-bonds). 3.6 amino acids per turn, pitch = 0.54nm, rise per residue = 0.15nm. Side chains projecting outward. Numbering of residues shown

options.B: 3.6 amino acids per turn

questions.question: Vitamin K is essential for:

options.B: Synthesis of clotting factors (II, VII, IX, X)

questions.question: Phosphorus in the body is found mainly in:

options.B: Bones and teeth (as hydroxyapatite) and in ATP, DNA, cell membranes

questions.question: Beri-beri is caused by deficiency of:

options.B: Vitamin B1 (Thiamine)

questions.question: The essential amino acids cannot be:

options.B: Synthesized by the body and must be obtained from diet

questions.question: The diagram shows the structure of haemoglobin. What level of protein structure does haemoglobin demonstrate?
questions.diagram_description: Diagram of haemoglobin molecule showing: 4 polypeptide chains (2 alpha chains + 2 beta chains in HbA), each chain separately folded (tertiary structure), each associated with one haem group. Four chains assembled together into one functional protein (quaternary structure). Arrows showing: primary (sequence), secondary (helical segments in each chain), tertiary (3D of each chain), quaternary (4-chain assembly)

options.B: Quaternary structure (4 polypeptide subunits assembled together)

questions.question: Vitamin A (retinol) deficiency causes:

options.B: Night blindness and xerophthalmia

questions.question: Magnesium is a cofactor for:

options.B: Many enzymes including ATPase, kinases, and stabilizes DNA/RNA structure

questions.question: The peptide bond is formed between:

options.B: Carboxyl group of one amino acid and amino group of next (–CO–NH– bond)

questions.question: Sodium and potassium are mainly involved in:

options.B: Maintaining membrane potential and osmotic balance

questions.question: The diagram shows water-soluble vs fat-soluble vitamins. Which water-soluble vitamin acts as antioxidant?
questions.diagram_description: Table/diagram showing vitamins classified by solubility: Fat-soluble (stored in liver/adipose – A, D, E, K). Water-soluble (not stored, excreted in urine – B complex: B1 thiamine, B2 riboflavin, B3 niacin, B5 pantothenic acid, B6 pyridoxine, B7 biotin, B9 folate, B12 cobalamin; and Vitamin C ascorbic acid). Functions listed for each. Vitamin C and Vitamin E labeled as antioxidants. Water-soluble vitamins highlighted

options.B: Vitamin C (ascorbic acid)

questions.question: Ferritin is a protein involved in:

options.B: Iron storage in liver and spleen

questions.question: Which vitamin is synthesized in the skin by UV radiation?

options.B: Vitamin D (cholecalciferol from 7-dehydrocholesterol)

questions.question: Protein denaturation is irreversible when:

options.B: Extensive conditions (high heat, extreme pH) cause aggregation and cannot be reversed
options.D: Urea at low concentration causes it

questions.question: Cobalamin (Vitamin B12) requires intrinsic factor for absorption in:

options.B: Ileum (intrinsic factor produced by parietal cells of stomach)

questions.question: The diagram shows enzyme-substrate interaction. What makes an enzyme specific for its substrate?
questions.diagram_description: Diagram showing enzyme active site complementarity: enzyme with specific 3D active site cleft. Correct substrate fits perfectly (induced fit model – enzyme shape adjusts slightly on binding). Enzyme-substrate complex formed. Products released, enzyme unchanged. Wrong substrate shown not fitting. Lock-and-key model (rigid) vs induced fit model (flexible) compared. Active site amino acids make specific interactions

options.B: Complementary 3D shape of active site to substrate (induced fit)

questions.question: Kwashiorkor is caused by:

options.B: Protein deficiency with adequate calorie intake

questions.question: Selenium is an essential mineral that functions as:

options.B: Component of antioxidant enzyme glutathione peroxidase

questions.question: Alpha keratin (protein of hair and nails) has:

options.B: Predominantly alpha helix structure with many disulfide cross-links

questions.question: Fluoride in teeth prevents dental caries by:

options.B: Converting hydroxyapatite to fluorapatite (more acid-resistant enamel)

questions.question: The secondary structure of proteins was described by:

options.B: Linus Pauling and Robert Corey (alpha helix and beta sheet)

questions.question: Copper deficiency can cause:

options.B: Anaemia and neurological problems (copper needed for ceruloplasmin, cytochrome c oxidase)

questions.question: Collagen is a structural protein that has:

options.B: Triple helix structure (three polypeptides wound together), rich in glycine, proline, hydroxyproline

questions.question: Vitamin E (tocopherol) functions as:

options.B: Antioxidant protecting cell membranes from lipid peroxidation

questions.question: Potassium deficiency (hypokalaemia) causes:

options.B: Muscle weakness and cardiac arrhythmias

questions.question: The diagram shows amino acid structure. Which part varies between different amino acids?
questions.diagram_description: General amino acid structure diagram showing: central alpha carbon (Cα), amino group (-NH2) on left, carboxyl group (-COOH) on right, hydrogen atom below, and R group (side chain) above. R group labeled as variable part. Example R groups shown: glycine (H), alanine (CH3), serine (CH2OH), cysteine (CH2SH), phenylalanine (benzyl group). Zwitterion form at physiological pH also shown

options.B: R group (side chain)

questions.question: Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) is important for:

options.B: Amino acid metabolism (transamination and decarboxylation reactions)

questions.question: The Sanger method determined the primary structure of:

options.B: Insulin (first protein to have its complete amino acid sequence determined, 1955)

questions.question: Chlorine in the body functions mainly as:

options.B: Electrolyte for osmotic balance, component of HCl in stomach

questions.question: Protein folding is driven by:

options.B: Thermodynamically favorable hydrophobic collapse and bond formation

questions.question: The diagram shows biotin (Vitamin B7) function. Biotin acts as coenzyme for:
questions.diagram_description: Diagram showing biotin structure (bicyclic ring system) and its role as cofactor: Biotin attached to enzyme via lysine residue (biocytin). Carboxylation reactions: Pyruvate + CO2 → Oxaloacetate (pyruvate carboxylase, biotin). Acetyl-CoA + CO2 → Malonyl-CoA (acetyl-CoA carboxylase for fatty acid synthesis, biotin). Biotin acts as CO2 carrier. Deficiency caused by raw egg white consumption (avidin binds biotin)

options.B: Carboxylation reactions (CO2 transfer)

questions.question: Marasmus is caused by:

options.B: Total calorie and protein deficiency (severe malnutrition)

questions.question: Haemosiderin is:

options.B: Iron storage protein complex (aggregated ferritin) in liver, spleen, bone marrow

questions.question: Which amino acid has an imidazole side chain important for enzyme active sites and acts as pH buffer?

options.B: Histidine (pKa ~6 – ideal buffer at physiological pH)

questions.question: Transferrin is the plasma protein that:

options.B: Transports iron in blood from intestine to tissues
